title: Hash browns
subtitle: Shredded potato patties fried crisp and golden
excerpt: The American diner breakfast potato: raw floury potatoes grated coarsely, squeezed very dry, seasoned, and pressed into patties that fry in oil and butter until the outside is a properly crisp, golden crust and the inside stays soft. The squeezing step is not optional.
sourceNotes: Hashed brown potatoes appear in Fannie Farmer's 'The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book' (1896, Project Gutenberg #7544), where she describes the preparation of diced or chopped cooked potato browned in fat. The shredded raw-potato version that became the American diner standard emerged in the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries as a quicker, no-cook preparation. Mrs Beeton's 'Book of Household Management' (1861, Project Gutenberg #10136) includes similar potato-frying preparations.

content.text: The entire technique of making a good hash brown is in the squeezing. Grated raw potato contains a large amount of water, and if that water is not removed before cooking, it will steam the patty from the inside instead of frying it. Use a clean kitchen cloth or a large handful of kitchen paper; wrap the grated potato tightly and twist or press hard until nothing more comes out. The potato will feel almost dry and will have reduced noticeably in volume. This is correct.

Use a floury potato variety, Maris Piper, King Edward, or Russet. Waxy varieties (Charlotte, Jersey) hold too much water and do not crisp properly. Season the squeezed potato with salt and black pepper, press into patties about 1.5 cm thick, and fry in a combination of oil and butter over medium-high heat. The patties need about 4 minutes per side; do not try to rush this by turning too early.

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: floury variety (Maris Piper, King Edward, or Russet), peeled and coarsely grated

content.text: Place the grated {{potato}} in the centre of a clean kitchen cloth or several layers of kitchen paper. Draw the corners together and twist tightly, squeezing out as much liquid as possible. Transfer the squeezed potato to a bowl. Season generously with salt and black pepper and onion powder if using.

Divide the potato into four equal portions and press each into a flat round patty about 1.5 cm thick. Melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in the cast-iron skillet over medium-high heat until foaming. Add the patties without crowding the pan. Press each one down firmly with a fish slice. Cook for 4 to 5 minutes without moving until the underside is a deep, even golden brown. Flip carefully and cook for a further 3 to 4 minutes until the second side matches. Drain briefly on kitchen paper and serve immediately.

items.fix: Squeeze the grated potato more aggressively; the squeezed ball should feel nearly dry when pressed between your palms
items.cause: Potato not squeezed dry enough; residual water steams rather than fries
items.symptom: Hash browns are soft and not crisp

items.fix: Press the patties firmly when you shape them. Press down again in the pan straight away. Do not turn until the bottom is set and golden. That takes at least 4 minutes.
items.cause: Potato not held together before frying, or turned too early
items.symptom: Patties fall apart in the pan

items.fix: Use medium-high heat and wait for a deep golden crust before turning; a lightly browned hash brown has not crisped properly
items.cause: Heat too low; not enough browning before turning
items.symptom: Hash browns are pale but soft inside

items.fix: Keep patties to 1.5 cm maximum thickness; cook until deeply golden on both sides to ensure the centre is fully cooked
items.cause: Raw starch not properly cooked through; patty too thick

content.text: Hashed potato dishes show up in American cooking from the late 1800s. The potato was a staple food, and short-order diner kitchens needed quick sides. The shredded-and-fried style became a standard diner breakfast by the early 1900s. Later it landed on every fast-food breakfast menu, which made it familiar all over the world. The good home version, made with floury potato squeezed dry and cooked in real butter, beats the version most people first taste.
